informal: Prove that if $f$ is holomorphic in the unit disc, bounded and not identically zero, and $z_{1}, z_{2}, \ldots, z_{n}, \ldots$ are its zeros $\left(\left|z_{k}\right|<1\right)$, then $\sum_{n}\left(1-\left|z_{n}\right|\right)<\infty$.
lean:
theorem Shakarchi_exercise_5_1_corrected
    (f : ℂ → ℂ) (_hf : DifferentiableOn ℂ f (ball 0 1))
    (_hb : Bornology.IsBounded (Set.range f)) (_h0 : f ≠ 0) (zeros : ℕ → ℂ)
    (_hz : Set.range zeros = {z | f z = 0 ∧ z ∈ (ball (0 : ℂ) 1)})
    (hsumm : Summable fun n : ℕ => (1 - ‖zeros n‖)) :
    ∃ (l : ℝ), Tendsto (fun n => ∑ i ∈ range n, (1 - ‖zeros i‖)) atTop (𝓝 l)
reasoning: The informal statement asks to prove that the sum ∑(1 − |z_n|) converges (i.e., that the sequence is summable). The Lean signature instead takes `hsumm : Summable (fun n => 1 − ‖zeros n‖)` as a hypothesis and merely concludes that the partial sums tend to some limit — which is an immediate triviality from `Summable`. Thus the Lean theorem assumes the very fact that was to be proved, and its actual content is vacuous compared to the informal claim. The substantive conclusion (summability of (1 − |z_n|) for zeros of a bounded holomorphic function on the disc) is missing, so the Lean version is strictly weaker than the informal statement.
